text: We have two ears and one mouth, so that we may listen twice as much as we speak.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.23

text: Better to trip with the feet than with the tongue.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.26

text: The most necessary kind of learning is to unlearn what is bad.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.23

text: A friend is a second self.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.23

text: The end is to live in agreement with nature; which is to live according to virtue, for nature leads us toward virtue.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.87

text: Virtue alone is sufficient for happiness; it needs nothing added to it from outside.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.127

text: All things are parts of one single system, which is called Nature. The life of man is a part of the whole; and a good life is one that is in harmony with its nature.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.88

text: The wise man alone is truly free. Every foolish man is a slave—slave to appetite, to fear, to anger, to his own passions, whichever master happens to drive him.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.121

text: If you lay violent hands on me, you may have my body, but my mind will remain with Antisthenes.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.3

text: The passions are judgements; and it is because they are false judgements about things indifferent that they grip us. Correct the judgement, and the passion dissolves.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.111

text: No loss should be more regrettable to us than losing our time; for it is irretrievable, unlike gold or land.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.24

text: Well-being is attained by little and little, yet it is no little thing itself.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.26

text: If you seek virtue, you need nothing else; but if you seek anything else, you will find neither it nor virtue.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.105

text: The things that are truly good are the virtues: prudence, justice, courage, temperance, and all that participates in them. The things that are truly bad are their opposites. Everything else is indifferent.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.102

text: Among indifferent things, some are to be preferred and others to be rejected; wealth and health are preferred, poverty and sickness rejected—yet none of them are good or evil.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.105

text: We ought not to do evil that good may come of it; for the end does not justify means that are contrary to virtue.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.128

text: Ask yourself at every moment: Is this the act of a free man or a slave? Free men act from reason and principle; slaves act from fear, habit, or appetite.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.121

text: It is better to live on a little with contentment than to have plenty without peace of mind.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.26

text: One thing alone makes the soul truly rich: to hold fast to what is honorable and to despise what only appears good.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.104

text: The divine fire—the Logos—pervades all things, binding together the world into one living rational whole. To act against reason is to act against God and against one's own deepest nature.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.134

text: Educate your children in philosophy, lest when they grow up they need consolation for misfortune. Then, if fortune is kind, they will be joyful; and if unkind, they will not be crushed.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.33

text: The good man is never alone; he carries with him all that is his—his virtue, his reason, and therefore all true wealth.
source: Fragment, via Diogenes Laërtius, Lives VII.125
